content:
# Exodus Chapter 4 — The EMET Bible (Literal)

**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios ~33r–33v (PDF pp. 64–65); Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 44–45 (LXX) | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_004_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_004_page_002.jpg`; `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/EXO_004_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_004_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

**1** And Mosheh answered and said: "And behold, they will not believe me, and they will not listen to my voice, for they will say: 'YHWH did not appear to you.'"

**2** And YHWH said to him: "What [is] this in your hand?" And he said: "A rod."

**3** And he said: "Cast it to the ground." And he cast it to the ground, and it became a nakhash [serpent]; and Mosheh fled from before it.

**4** And YHWH said to Mosheh: "Stretch out your hand and seize it by its tail." And he stretched out his hand and took-hold of it, and it became a rod in his palm.

**5** "In-order-that they may believe that YHWH, the God of their fathers, appeared to you -- the God of Avraham, the God of Yitshaq, and the God of Ya'aqov."

**6** And YHWH said to him again: "Bring now your hand into your bosom." And he brought his hand into his bosom; and he brought it out, and behold, his hand [was] leprous as snow.

**7** And he said: "Return your hand into your bosom." And he returned his hand into his bosom; and he brought it out from his bosom, and behold, it had returned as his [other] flesh.

**8** "And it shall be, if they will not believe you and will not listen to the voice of the first sign, then they will believe the voice of the latter sign.

**9** And it shall be, if they will not believe even these two signs, and will not listen to your voice, then you shall take from the waters of the Ye'or [Nile] and pour [them] upon the dry-ground; and the waters which you take from the Ye'or shall become blood upon the dry-ground."

**10** And Mosheh said to YHWH: "Please, my Lord, I [am] not a man of words, neither from yesterday nor from the-day-before nor from-then [you have] spoken to your servant, for heavy of mouth and heavy of tongue [am] I."

**11** And YHWH said to him: "Who placed a mouth for man? Or who makes [him] mute, or deaf, or seeing, or blind? [Is it] not I, YHWH?

**12** And now, go, and I Myself will be with your mouth, and I will instruct you [in] what you shall speak."

**13** And he said: "Please, my Lord, send now by the hand [of whomever] you will send."

**14** And the anger of YHWH burned against Mosheh, and he said: "[Is there] not Aharon your brother, the Levi? I know that speaking he will speak -- he. And also, behold, he [is] going-out to meet you, and he will see you and he will rejoice in his heart.

**15** And you shall speak to him and you shall put the words in his mouth; and I Myself will be with your mouth and with his mouth, and I will instruct you [in] what you shall do.

**16** And he shall speak for you to the people; and it shall be [that] he -- he shall be to you as a mouth, and you -- you shall be to him as God.

**17** And this rod you shall take in your hand, with which you shall do the signs."

**18** And Mosheh went and returned to Yeter his father-in-law, and he said to him: "Let me go, please, and let me return to my brothers who [are] in Mitsrayim, and let me see whether they [are] still alive." And Yitro said to Mosheh: "Go in peace."

**19** And YHWH said to Mosheh in Midyan: "Go, return [to] Mitsrayim, for all the men who were seeking your life have died."

**20** And Mosheh took his wife and his sons and he set them upon the donkey, and he returned [to] the land of Mitsrayim. And Mosheh took the rod of God in his hand.

**21** And YHWH said to Mosheh: "When you go to return [to] Mitsrayim, see all the wonders which I have put in your hand, and you shall do them before Par'oh; and I -- I will strengthen his heart, and he will not send the people out.

**22** And you shall say to Par'oh: 'Thus says YHWH: My son, my firstborn, [is] Yisra'el.

**23** And I say to you: Send out my son and let him serve me; and [if] you refuse to send him out -- behold, I [am about to] kill your son, your firstborn.'"

**24** And it was on the way, at the lodging-place, and YHWH met him and sought to kill him.

**25** And Tsipporah took a flint and she cut-off the foreskin of her son, and she caused-[it]-to-touch his feet; and she said: "For a hatan damim [bridegroom of blood] [are] you to me."

**26** And he withdrew from him. Then she said: "A hatan damim [bridegroom of blood] -- for the mulot [circumcisions]."

**27** And YHWH said to Aharon: "Go to the wilderness to meet Mosheh." And he went and he met him at the mountain of God, and he kissed him.

**28** And Mosheh told Aharon all the words of YHWH with which he had sent him, and all the signs with which he had commanded him.

**29** And Mosheh and Aharon went, and they gathered all the elders of the sons of Yisra'el.

**30** And Aharon spoke all the words which YHWH had spoken to Mosheh, and he did the signs before the eyes of the people.

**31** And the people believed; and they heard that YHWH had visited the sons of Yisra'el, and that he had seen their affliction; and they bowed and they prostrated-themselves.

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

### Manuscript Observations (Leningrad Codex B19A)

1. **v.1 -- Mosheh's fourth objection:** This verse begins the fourth and final objection of Mosheh in his dialogue with YHWH (following 3:11, 3:13, and the implicit resistance throughout 3:19-22). The Leningrad Codex reads va-ya'an mosheh va-yomer ("and Mosheh answered and said") with the standard narrative wayyiqtol chain. The verb 'anah ("to answer") here functions as a response to the divine commission, not to a specific question. The LXX (Vaticanus p. 44) reads apekrithe de Mouses kai eipen ("and Mosheh answered and said"), closely matching the Hebrew. Note that Mosheh's concern is not merely personal reluctance but theological: the people will deny the theophany itself -- "YHWH did not appear to you."

2. **v.2 -- "What [is] this in your hand?" (mah zeh be-yadekha):** The Leningrad Codex reads the question without an interrogative particle he -- the interrogative force comes from mah ("what"). YHWH's question is pedagogical, not informational: he knows what Mosheh holds. The word matteh ("rod/staff") denotes a shepherd's staff and will become the instrument of the signs throughout the Exodus narrative. The LXX reads ti estin touto to en te cheiri sou ("what is this thing in your hand"), expanding slightly with the demonstrative touto.

3. **v.3 -- nakhash (serpent):** The Leningrad Codex reads va-yehi le-nakhash ("and it became a serpent"). The word nakhash is the common word for serpent, the same word used in Genesis 3:1 for the serpent in the garden. The LXX reads egeneto ophis ("it became a serpent"). Notably, when this sign is performed before Par'oh in 7:9-10, a different Hebrew word is used: tannin ("sea-serpent, dragon, crocodile"). The Leningrad Codex distinction between nakhash here and tannin in 7:9 is consistent and deliberate. The LXX also maintains this distinction (ophis here vs. drakon in 7:9).

4. **v.4 -- "seize it by its tail" (ehoz bi-zenavo):** The imperative ehoz (from '-h-z, "to seize, grasp") with the prepositional phrase bi-zenavo ("by its tail"). Grasping a serpent by its tail is the most dangerous way to handle it -- it leaves the head free to strike. The command thus requires an act of faith against instinct. The LXX reads ekteinon ten cheira kai epilabou tes kerkeu ("stretch out the hand and take hold of the tail").

5. **v.6 -- "leprous as snow" (metsora'at ka-shaleg):** The Leningrad Codex reads metsora'at with the feminine pual participle of ts-r-' ("to be struck with skin disease"). The comparison ka-shaleg ("as snow") indicates the whiteness of the affliction. The term tsara'at in the Hebrew Bible covers a range of skin conditions broader than modern leprosy (Hansen's disease). The LXX renders leprousa hosei chion ("leprous as snow"), using the same disease terminology.

6. **v.9 -- "the waters of the Ye'or" (mi-mey ha-ye'or):** The Leningrad Codex reads ye'or, a loanword from Egyptian (itrw/i'rw), referring specifically to the Nile River. This word is used almost exclusively for the Nile in the Hebrew Bible. The LXX renders tou hudatos tou potamou ("the water of the river"), not using a proper name. The three signs form an escalating sequence: rod-to-serpent (power over nature), leprous hand (power over the body), water-to-blood (power over Egypt's life-source).

7. **v.10 -- "heavy of mouth and heavy of tongue" (kevad peh u-khvad lashon):** The Leningrad Codex reads kevad peh u-khvad lashon clearly. The adjective kavod/kavev ("heavy, weighty") is from the root k-v-d, the same root that gives us kavod ("glory, heaviness"). Mosheh's "heavy mouth and heavy tongue" describe some form of speech difficulty. The exact nature is debated: a stutter, a lisp, slowness of speech, or simply lack of eloquence. The LXX renders ischophonos kai braduglossos ("weak-voiced and slow-tongued"), interpreting the "heaviness" as weakness and slowness. The word gam ("also, even") before mi-temol ("from yesterday") and gam before mi-shilshom ("from the day before") and gam before me-az ("from then") creates a triple emphasis: this is not a new problem and has not been fixed by the theophany. The phrase me-az dabberekha el avdekha ("from then [you have been] speaking to your servant") indicates that even after YHWH began speaking to him, Mosheh's speech impediment persists.

8. **v.11 -- "Who placed a mouth for man?" (mi sam peh la-adam):** YHWH's response is a creation-theology argument: the one who created the mouth can enable it to speak. The Leningrad Codex reads a series of rhetorical questions using the interrogative mi ("who") and the particle 'o ("or"). The four conditions listed -- illem (mute), heresh (deaf), piqqeah (seeing/clear-sighted), 'ivver (blind) -- form two contrasting pairs. The LXX renders tis edoken stoma anthropo kai tis epoiese duskopon kai kophon kai bleponta kai tuphlon ("who gave a mouth to man, and who made [one] hard-of-hearing and deaf and seeing and blind").

9. **v.13 -- "send by the hand [of whomever] you will send" (shelah na be-yad tishlah):** This is Mosheh's fifth and final objection, and the most audacious -- a barely veiled request to be excused entirely. The Leningrad Codex reads shelah na be-yad tishlah -- literally "send, please, by the hand you-will-send," leaving the object unspecified. The indefiniteness is the point: "send anyone else." The LXX reads procheirisai dunamenon allon hon apostereis ("appoint someone else able whom you will send"), making explicit what the Hebrew leaves elliptical. This is the only objection that provokes divine anger (v. 14).

10. **v.14 -- "the anger of YHWH burned" (va-yihar af YHWH):** The Leningrad Codex reads va-yihar af yhwh be-mosheh. The idiom yihar af (literally "the nose/nostril burned") is the standard Hebrew expression for anger -- the physiological image of flaring nostrils. Despite this anger, YHWH's response is not punishment but provision: he gives Mosheh a spokesman. The LXX reads kai ethumotheis orge kurios epi Mousen ("and having been angered with anger, the Lord [was angry] at Mosheh"), intensifying with the cognate construction ethumotheis orge.

11. **v.14 -- "Aharon your brother, the Levi" (aharon akhikha ha-levi):** The Leningrad Codex reads ha-levi with the definite article, designating Aharon by his tribal identity. This is the first mention of Aharon in Exodus. The phrase dabber yedabber hu' ("speaking he will speak -- he") uses the infinitive absolute construction with the independent pronoun hu' ("he") for triple emphasis: Aharon is emphatically a capable speaker. The LXX reads Aaron ho adelphos sou ho Leuites ("Aaron your brother the Levite").

12. **v.16 -- "he shall be to you as a mouth... you shall be to him as God" (hu' yihyeh lekha le-peh... ve-attah tihyeh lo le-lohim):** The Leningrad Codex reads le-peh ("as a mouth," i.e., spokesman) and le-lohim ("as God," i.e., the authoritative source). This establishes the prophetic paradigm: the prophet is the "mouth" of God, speaking not his own words but those placed in him. Mosheh stands in the position of God relative to Aharon; Aharon stands as Mosheh's prophet relative to the people. The LXX reads autos soi lalesei pros ton laon kai autos estai sou stoma su de ese auto ta pros ton theon ("he shall speak for you to the people, and he shall be your mouth, and you shall be to him things pertaining to God"), expanding the concise Hebrew.

13. **v.18 -- "Yeter" (yeter):** The Leningrad Codex reads yeter khotno ("Yeter his father-in-law"), not Yitro as in 3:1. This is a variant form of the same name (yeter = "abundance, remnant" from y-t-r, the same root as yitro). Later in the same verse, the text switches to Yitro: va-yomer yitro le-mosheh ("and Yitro said to Mosheh"). The Leningrad Codex preserves both forms within a single verse. The LXX reads Iothor consistently in both occurrences, harmonizing the variation. The EMET preserves the Leningrad Codex's distinction, rendering the first as Yeter and the second as Yitro.

14. **v.19 -- "all the men seeking your life have died" (ki metu kol ha-anashim ha-mevaqeshim et nafshekha):** The Leningrad Codex reads metu (qal perfect of m-w-t, "they have died"). This echoes the language that will later be used of Mosheh himself: "after the death of Mosheh" (Deuteronomy 34:5). The phrasing is echoed in Matthew 2:20 regarding Joseph's return from Egypt: tethnēkasin gar hoi zētountes tēn psuchēn tou paidiou ("for those seeking the life of the child have died"). The LXX reads tethnēkasin gar pantes hoi zētountes sou tēn psuchēn ("for all those seeking your life have died"), closely matching the Hebrew.

15. **v.20 -- "his sons" (banav):** The Leningrad Codex reads banav (plural, "his sons"), though only one son (Gershom) has been named so far (2:22). A second son, Eli'ezer, is named in 18:4. Some scholars see this as evidence that the narrative traditions have been combined. The LXX reads ta paidia ("the children"), using a neuter plural. The phrase mateh ha-elohim ("the rod of God") now elevates the shepherd's staff of v. 2 -- it has become the instrument of divine power.

16. **v.21 -- "I will strengthen his heart" (va-ani ahazzeq et libbo):** The Leningrad Codex reads ahazzeq (piel imperfect of h-z-q, "to make strong, harden"). This is the first occurrence of the "hardening of Par'oh's heart" motif, which will recur throughout Exodus in three different verbal forms: h-z-q (to strengthen), k-v-d (to make heavy), and q-sh-h (to make hard). The theological question of divine hardening versus human free will is one of the great interpretive challenges of Exodus. Notably, in the early plagues Par'oh hardens his own heart; only later does YHWH harden it. Here YHWH announces his intention prospectively. The LXX reads ego de sklēruno tēn kardian autou ("but I will harden his heart"), using sklērunō ("to make hard/stubborn"), which carries a stronger negative connotation than the Hebrew h-z-q ("to strengthen").

17. **v.22 -- "My son, my firstborn, Yisra'el" (beni bekhori yisra'el):** The Leningrad Codex reads beni bekhori yisra'el -- three nouns in apposition. This is the first time in the Torah that Yisra'el is called YHWH's "son" and "firstborn." The designation bekhor ("firstborn") implies both special status and the rights of inheritance. The LXX reads huios prototokos mou Israel ("my firstborn son, Israel"). This verse establishes the theological foundation for v. 23: because Yisra'el is YHWH's firstborn, the killing of Par'oh's firstborn is measure-for-measure justice (middah ke-neged middah).

18. **vv.24-26 -- The hatan damim passage:** This is one of the most obscure and debated passages in the entire Torah. The Leningrad Codex text is clear, but the referents of the pronouns are deeply ambiguous.

    **v.24 -- "YHWH met him and sought to kill him" (va-yifgeshehu YHWH va-yevaqesh hamito):** The Leningrad Codex reads va-yifgeshehu (wayyiqtol of p-g-sh with 3ms suffix, "and he met him"). The subject is YHWH. The verb biqesh ("to seek") with the infinitive construct hamito ("to kill him") creates the astounding claim that YHWH sought to kill -- but whom? The 3ms suffix "him" most naturally refers to Mosheh, the last named masculine subject. But some interpreters argue it refers to the son. The passage stands in stark tension with the preceding commission: YHWH has just sent Mosheh to deliver Yisra'el, and now seeks to kill him on the way. The LXX reads sunanteisen auto aggelos kuriou kai ezetei auton apokteinai ("an angel of the Lord met him and sought to kill him"), substituting "angel of the Lord" for YHWH -- a theological softening of the stark anthropomorphism.

    **v.25 -- "she caused-[it]-to-touch his feet" (va-tagga' le-raglav):** The Leningrad Codex reads va-tagga' (hiphil wayyiqtol of n-g-', "to touch, strike") le-raglav ("to his feet"). Tsipporah takes a tsur ("flint") and cuts (va-tikhrot) the 'orlat ("foreskin") of her son (benah). She then touches "his feet" with the foreskin -- but whose feet? The 3ms suffix raglav could refer to Mosheh, to the son, or (in some interpretations) to the divine attacker. The word "feet" (raglayim) is sometimes a euphemism for genitals in the Hebrew Bible (cf. Isaiah 7:20; Ruth 3:7; Judges 3:24). She then declares: ki hatan damim attah li ("for a bridegroom/kinsman of blood you-[are] to me"). The LXX renders kai prosepesen pros tous podas kai eipen ("and she fell before [his] feet and said"), reading the verb differently (prospipto, "to fall before" rather than "to touch"). The LXX also reads: esthe to haima tes peritomes tou paidiou mou ("the blood of the circumcision of my child has stood/stopped"), a significantly different text from the MT.

    **v.26 -- "a hatan damim for the mulot" (hatan damim la-mulot):** The Leningrad Codex reads va-yiref mimmennu ("and he withdrew from him") -- the subject (YHWH or the divine threat) released the attacked party. Then 'az amerah hatan damim la-mulot ("then she said: 'a bridegroom of blood -- for the circumcisions'"). The word mulot (plural of mulah, from m-w-l, "to circumcise") is found only here in the Hebrew Bible. The plural is enigmatic -- why "circumcisions" (plural)? Possible explanations: (a) she refers to circumcision as a category/practice; (b) there were two sons who needed circumcision; (c) the plural is intensive/abstract. The phrase hatan damim is itself unique to this passage. The word hatan can mean "bridegroom," "son-in-law," or "one who is related by marriage" (from h-t-n, "to become a son-in-law"). In Arabic, the cognate root khtn means "to circumcise," suggesting a deep etymological connection between circumcision and marriage/kinship. Thus hatan damim may mean "one who is a blood-relative through circumcision" or "a circumcision-bridegroom." The entire passage appears to be an etiological narrative explaining the connection between circumcision, blood, marriage, and covenant protection. The LXX's rendering departs substantially from the MT here, and the Vaticanus text (p. 44, bottom of col. 2 into col. 3) shows: estē to haima tēs peritomēs tou paidiou mou ("the blood of my child's circumcision has stopped").

19. **v.27 -- "the mountain of God" (har ha-elohim):** The Leningrad Codex reads va-yipgeshehu be-har ha-elohim va-yishshaq lo ("and he met him at the mountain of God and he kissed him"). Aharon meets Mosheh at Horev/Sinai, the same mountain where the burning bush theophany occurred (3:1). The kiss is a standard greeting between kinsmen in the ancient Near East. The LXX reads en to orei tou theou ("at the mountain of God") and katefilēsen auton ("he kissed him").

20. **v.31 -- "they bowed and they prostrated-themselves" (va-yiqqedu va-yishtahavu):** The Leningrad Codex reads two distinct verbs: qadad ("to bow the head") and hishtahavah ("to prostrate oneself"). These are the standard paired verbs for worship/homage in the Hebrew Bible, indicating a sequence of physical actions: first bowing the head, then full prostration. The response of the people encompasses both belief (va-ya'amen, v. 31a) and worship. The verb paqad ("visited") echoes 3:16, fulfilling YHWH's instruction: "I have surely visited you" (paqod paqadti). The LXX reads kai chareis to laos kai proskunēsen ("and the people rejoiced and worshipped"), adding "rejoiced" which is not in the MT.

### Key Theological and Interpretive Observations

**The Five Objections of Mosheh (3:11 -- 4:17):** Exodus 4 contains the final three of Mosheh's five objections to his divine commission:
- 3:11 -- "Who am I?" (inadequacy of person)
- 3:13 -- "What is his name?" (inadequacy of knowledge)
- 4:1 -- "They will not believe me" (inadequacy of authority)
- 4:10 -- "I am not a man of words" (inadequacy of speech)
- 4:13 -- "Send someone else" (outright refusal)

Each objection is met with a divine response, but the fifth provokes anger. The structure reveals a progressive hardening of Mosheh's resistance, mirroring ironically the hardening of Par'oh's heart that YHWH announces in v. 21.

**The Three Signs (vv. 2-9):** The signs form a structured sequence: (1) rod to serpent -- power over nature; (2) leprous hand -- power over the human body; (3) water to blood -- power over Egypt's life-source. The first two are performed; the third is described but not enacted until the first plague (7:14-24). Notably, the signs are given to convince the Israelites, not Par'oh -- they address the specific objection of v. 1.

**The hatan damim Crisis (vv. 24-26):** This remains one of the most discussed passages in biblical scholarship. Several interpretive frameworks:
- **Covenantal:** Mosheh (or his son) was uncircumcised, violating the Abrahamic covenant (Genesis 17). YHWH's attack enforces the covenant obligation before Mosheh can serve as covenant mediator.
- **Apotropaic:** Tsipporah's emergency circumcision and blood-application functions as a protective rite, warding off the divine threat through blood -- prefiguring the Passover blood on the doorposts (12:13, 22-23).
- **Etiological:** The passage preserves an ancient tradition linking circumcision, blood, and marital/kinship bonds (the hatan damim designation).
- **Narrative:** The passage creates a micro-Passover: a firstborn is threatened with death, blood is applied, the destroyer withdraws. This foreshadows the entire Exodus narrative in miniature.

The EMET preserves every word and its ambiguities. The pronouns are left as the Hebrew has them -- unresolved -- because the text itself does not resolve them.

**The Prophetic Paradigm (vv. 15-16):** The Mosheh-Aharon relationship establishes the definitive model for prophecy in the Hebrew Bible. Mosheh is "as God" (le-lohim) to Aharon; Aharon is "a mouth" (le-peh) for Mosheh. This is made explicit in 7:1: "See, I have made you God to Par'oh, and Aharon your brother shall be your prophet." The prophet does not originate the message but receives and transmits it.

**"My son, my firstborn" (v. 22):** The declaration of Yisra'el as YHWH's firstborn establishes the theological logic of the entire plague narrative and especially the tenth plague. The killing of Egypt's firstborn is not arbitrary divine violence but covenant enforcement: Par'oh enslaves YHWH's firstborn, so YHWH takes Par'oh's firstborn. The measure-for-measure principle (middah ke-neged middah) governs the divine justice.

### Vaticanus LXX Comparison Summary

The Vaticanus manuscript (Vat.gr.1209, pp. 44-45) preserves Exodus 4 in continuous Greek uncial across three columns per page. The chapter continues from Exodus 3 on page 44 and extends onto page 45 (marked CAP. L in the Vaticanus numbering system at the top of the page). The most significant LXX divergences from the MT in this chapter are:

- **v.3:** LXX reads ophis ("serpent"), matching MT's nakhash. Both traditions preserve the distinction from tannin/drakon in 7:9.
- **v.10:** LXX renders kevad peh u-khvad lashon as ischophonos kai braduglossos ("weak-voiced and slow-tongued"), interpreting "heaviness" as deficiency rather than preserving the literal metaphor.
- **v.13:** LXX expands Mosheh's elliptical refusal into an explicit request: "appoint another capable person."
- **v.14:** LXX intensifies the anger formula with a cognate construction (ethumotheis orge).
- **v.18:** LXX harmonizes both names to Iothor, eliminating the Yeter/Yitro variation preserved in the MT.
- **vv.24-26:** The most significant divergence in the chapter. LXX substitutes "angel of the Lord" for YHWH as the attacker (theological distancing). LXX reads Tsipporah's declaration as concerning "the blood of the circumcision of my child" rather than the enigmatic "bridegroom of blood." The Vaticanus text here represents a substantially different interpretive tradition from the MT.
- **v.21:** LXX renders ahazzeq ("I will strengthen") as sklēruno ("I will harden"), introducing a more negative connotation to the hardening motif than the Hebrew warrants.
- **v.24:** LXX's substitution of aggelos kuriou for YHWH is consistent with its broader tendency to buffer direct divine anthropomorphism, seen also in passages like Genesis 32:25-31.
- **v.31:** LXX adds chareis ("rejoiced") to the people's response, which has no equivalent in the MT. The Vaticanus text reads kai chareis to laos edakrusen kai proskunēsen ("and the people rejoiced and wept and worshipped") in some readings, though the Vaticanus hand at p. 45 is consistent with the shorter reading.

The Vaticanus text of Exodus 4 is well-preserved with no significant lacunae on pages 44-45. The transition from page 44 to page 45 occurs during the latter portion of the chapter. Column spacing remains regular, and the uncial hand is clear and consistent throughout.

-- EMET
